question: Which keyword establishes an inheritance relationship between two classes in Java?
correct: extends
incorrect: implements
inherits
super
feedback: class Dog extends Animal declares that Dog is a subclass of Animal. Dog inherits all non-private fields and methods from Animal. implements is for interfaces, not class inheritance.

question: What does the super() call inside a subclass constructor do?
correct: Invokes the parent class's constructor to initialize inherited fields
incorrect: Creates a second copy of the parent class object
Overrides the parent class's default constructor
Returns the parent class object to the caller
feedback: super() must be the first statement in a subclass constructor. If omitted, Java inserts super() automatically â€” but only if the parent has a no-argument constructor. If the parent requires arguments, super(...) must be called explicitly.

question: What does it mean to override a method in Java?
correct: A subclass provides its own implementation of a method defined in a parent class, using the same signature
incorrect: A subclass adds extra parameters to a parent's method
A class defines two methods with the same name but different parameter lists
A subclass hides the parent method by declaring a field with the same name
feedback: Method overriding: same name, same parameter types, same (or covariant) return type, in a subclass. The @Override annotation tells the compiler to verify this. Overloading (different parameters) is a separate concept.

question: What is polymorphism in Java?
correct: Using a parent-type reference to call methods that are dynamically dispatched to the actual subclass implementation
incorrect: Creating multiple constructors with different parameter lists
Defining the same method name in two unrelated classes
Casting an object from one type to another
feedback: Polymorphism allows Animal a = new Dog(); When a.speak() is called, Java calls Dog's speak(), not Animal's. The actual type at runtime (Dog) determines which method runs, not the declared type (Animal).

question: What does the abstract keyword mean when applied to a class?
correct: The class cannot be instantiated directly; it must be subclassed
incorrect: The class has no methods or fields
All methods in the class are automatically hidden from subclasses
The class can only be used as a parameter type, not as a variable type
feedback: An abstract class cannot be instantiated with new â€” you can only create objects from its concrete subclasses. Abstract classes often define abstract methods (no body) that subclasses must implement.

question: What is an interface in Java?
correct: A contract specifying method signatures that implementing classes must provide, with no constructor or instance fields
incorrect: A class that can be instantiated directly to provide default behavior
A collection of static methods grouped under a common name
A class that automatically generates getters and setters
feedback: An interface declares what a class can do, not how. Implementing a class must provide concrete implementations for all abstract methods. Interfaces support multiple implementation: class Dog implements Animal, Trainable is valid.

question: What does the instanceof operator do?
correct: Returns true if the object on the left is an instance of the type on the right (or a subtype)
incorrect: Casts the object to the specified type
Returns the class name of the object as a String
Throws an exception if the types are incompatible
feedback: instanceof is a boolean check: if (animal instanceof Dog). It is used before a downcast to avoid ClassCastException. A Dog object returns true for instanceof Dog, instanceof Animal, and instanceof Object.

question: What does the final keyword mean when applied to a class?
correct: The class cannot be extended (subclassed)
incorrect: The class cannot have any instance fields
The class can only be instantiated once
All methods in the class are implicitly static
feedback: final prevents extension: class String in Java is final, so you cannot create a subclass of String. final on a method prevents overriding. final on a variable prevents reassignment after initialization.

question: What is dynamic method dispatch in Java?
correct: Java determines which overridden method to call based on the actual runtime type of the object, not the declared reference type
incorrect: Java chooses the fastest method implementation at compile time
Java calls all matching methods in the inheritance hierarchy
Java delays method execution until the program is idle
feedback: With Animal a = new Dog(); the declared (static) type is Animal, but when a.speak() is called, Java looks at the actual (dynamic) type â€” Dog â€” at runtime and calls Dog's speak(). This is the mechanism behind polymorphism.

question: What is upcasting in Java?
correct: Treating a subclass object as an instance of its parent type â€” always safe and implicit
incorrect: Converting a parent type reference to a subclass type â€” requires an explicit cast
Calling a parent class method from inside a subclass
Widening a numeric primitive to a larger type
feedback: Upcasting is automatic: Animal a = new Dog(); is always safe because every Dog is-an Animal. Downcasting goes the other way (Dog d = (Dog) a;) and requires an explicit cast and is only safe when the runtime type actually is Dog.

question: Which claims about method overriding are correct?
correct: The overriding method must have the same name and parameter list as the parent method
The @Override annotation causes the compiler to verify the override is valid
An overriding method can have the same or a covariant (narrower) return type
incorrect: An overriding method can declare additional checked exceptions not declared by the parent
Overriding and overloading are the same thing
feedback: A valid override: same name, same parameters, same or covariant return type. Overriding a method cannot declare broader checked exceptions than the parent. Overloading is different parameters â€” completely separate concept.

question: Which claims about abstract classes are correct?
correct: An abstract class can contain both abstract methods (no body) and concrete methods (with body)
Attempting to instantiate an abstract class directly causes a compilation error
A subclass must implement all abstract methods or also be declared abstract
incorrect: An abstract class cannot have any constructors
An abstract class cannot have any fields
feedback: Abstract classes can have constructors (called via super()), fields, concrete methods, and abstract methods. They cannot be instantiated directly. A concrete subclass must implement all inherited abstract methods.

question: Which claims about interfaces are correct?
correct: A class can implement multiple interfaces
Interface methods are public and abstract by default (unless default or static)
Interfaces cannot be instantiated directly
incorrect: A class can only implement one interface at a time
Interfaces can declare instance fields to share state between implementing classes
feedback: Multiple interface implementation (implements A, B, C) is one of Java's key features. Interface methods are implicitly public abstract unless declared default or static. Interfaces can have constants (static final fields) but not mutable instance state.

question: Which claims about the super keyword are correct?
correct: super() calls the parent class's constructor and must be the first statement in the subclass constructor
super.methodName() explicitly calls the parent class's version of an overridden method
super can only be used inside a subclass
incorrect: super refers to an object of the parent class that is stored as a field
super() can appear anywhere in a constructor, as long as it is before any return statement
feedback: super is a reference to the parent portion of the current object, not a separate object. super() in a constructor must be the very first statement. super.method() calls the parent's implementation.

question: Which claims about the final keyword are correct?
correct: A final variable cannot be reassigned after its initial assignment
A final method cannot be overridden in a subclass
A final class cannot be extended
incorrect: A final class cannot have any methods
A final method cannot be called from outside the class
feedback: final has the same restriction idea in all three contexts: no reassignment (variable), no overriding (method), no extending (class). It does not affect visibility (public/private) or callability.

question: Which claims about static methods and fields are correct?
correct: Static members belong to the class, not to any individual object
Static methods cannot access instance fields or call instance methods directly
Static fields are shared across all instances of the class
incorrect: Static methods can use the this keyword to refer to the current instance
Each object has its own copy of a static field
feedback: Static members are class-level. Since no specific instance exists when a static method runs, this is unavailable and instance members cannot be accessed. Changing a static field from any object or from the class itself changes it for everyone.

question: Which claims about the protected access modifier are correct?
correct: Protected members are visible to subclasses regardless of which package they are in
Protected members are also visible to all classes in the same package
Protected is less restrictive than private but more restrictive than public
incorrect: Protected members are visible only to subclasses â€” not to other classes in the same package
Protected and default (package-private) access have the same scope
feedback: protected = same package + subclasses (anywhere). default (no modifier) = same package only (no subclasses in other packages). protected is strictly broader than default.

question: In Java, a subclass declares its parent using the keyword ___.
correct: extends
feedback: class Cat extends Animal { } declares Cat as a subclass of Animal. Use implements for interfaces (class Cat implements Runnable).

question: The keyword used to call the parent class's constructor from inside a subclass constructor is ___.
correct: super
feedback: super() invokes the parent constructor and must be the first statement. It initializes the inherited part of the object before the subclass constructor continues.

question: A class that cannot be instantiated directly and may contain abstract methods is declared with the keyword ___.
correct: abstract
feedback: abstract class Shape cannot be created with new Shape(). Its subclasses (Circle, Rectangle) can be instantiated if they implement all abstract methods.

question: A class that cannot be subclassed is declared with the keyword ___.
correct: final
feedback: final class String in Java cannot be extended. This prevents subclasses from altering the class's behavior, which is important for security and correctness.

question: The operator used to check whether an object is an instance of a given type at runtime is ___.
correct: instanceof
feedback: if (animal instanceof Dog) returns true if animal holds a Dog object (or a subclass of Dog). Always use instanceof before a downcast to avoid ClassCastException.

question: An abstract method in Java has no ___ â€” it only declares the signature.
correct: body
feedback: Abstract methods are declared without curly braces: public abstract void makeSound(); Concrete subclasses must provide the implementation. This forces subclasses to define specific behavior.

question: Match each inheritance keyword to its purpose.

pairs.definition: Declares that a class inherits from a parent class
Declares that a class fulfills an interface contract
References the parent class's constructor or methods
Marks a class or method as incomplete â€” cannot be instantiated directly

feedback: extends is for class-to-class; implements is for class-to-interface. super accesses the parent. abstract forces subclasses to provide implementation.

question: Match each concept to whether it describes overriding or overloading.

pairs.term: Same name, same parameters, in a subclass
pairs.definition: Overriding â€” the subclass replaces the parent's behavior

pairs.term: Same name, different parameters, same class
pairs.definition: Overloading â€” multiple versions of a method with different signatures
Overriding â€” the annotation asks the compiler to verify the signature
Overriding â€” the actual runtime type determines which version runs

feedback: Override = subclass, same signature, runtime resolution. Overload = same class, different signature, compile-time resolution. They are unrelated concepts that share the same method name.

question: Match each OOP mechanism to the problem it solves.

pairs.definition: Shares common code among related classes while forcing specific behavior to be implemented by subclasses
Defines a contract that unrelated classes can fulfill, enabling polymorphism without inheritance
Prevents unintended modification of critical class behavior through subclassing
Safely checks type before downcasting to avoid ClassCastException

feedback: Use abstract class when classes share implementation; use interface when they just share a contract. final protects against unsafe extension. instanceof is the safe-cast guard.

question: Match each term to the casting scenario it describes.

pairs.definition: Treating a subclass as its parent type â€” automatic and always safe
Treating a parent reference as a specific subclass â€” explicit cast, only safe when the runtime type matches
The exception thrown when a downcast fails because the runtime type is incompatible
The operator used to verify a type before performing a downcast

feedback: Upcasting: Animal a = new Dog() â€” safe and automatic. Downcasting: Dog d = (Dog) a â€” explicit, must check with instanceof first. A failed downcast throws ClassCastException at runtime.

question: What does this overriding example print? (Captain Doran is demonstrating polymorphic dispatch.)
correct: woof
feedback: Even though the variable a is declared as Animal, the object is actually a Dog. Java dispatches to the Dog's sound() override at runtime. This is dynamic dispatch (runtime polymorphism).

question: What does this super call print?
correct: shape+circle
feedback: Circle.describe() calls super.describe() first, which returns "shape". It then concatenates "+circle" to get "shape+circle". super lets a subclass extend rather than fully replace its parent's behavior.

question: What does this abstract class example print?
correct: vehicle with 2 wheels
feedback: describe() is a concrete method on the abstract class that calls wheels(), which is abstract. When called on a Bike instance, dynamic dispatch runs Bike.wheels() returning 2, so the full string is "vehicle with 2 wheels".

question: Write the class header (with opening brace) for a class named Dog that extends the class Animal.
correct: class Dog extends Animal {
public class Dog extends Animal {
class Dog extends Animal
public class Dog extends Animal
feedback: class Dog extends Animal — extends establishes single inheritance. Dog inherits all non-private fields and methods of Animal and can override them. Java classes can extend only one superclass.

question: Write the class header (with opening brace) for a class named Circle that implements the interface Drawable.
correct: class Circle implements Drawable {
public class Circle implements Drawable {
class Circle implements Drawable
public class Circle implements Drawable
feedback: class Circle implements Drawable — implements is used for interfaces, not extends. A class can implement multiple interfaces, separated by commas. Circle must provide a body for every abstract method Drawable declares.

question: Inside a subclass constructor, write the Java statement that calls the parent class constructor with the single argument name.
correct: super(name);
feedback: super(name); invokes the parent class constructor. It must be the first statement in the subclass constructor. If omitted and the parent has no no-arg constructor, the code will not compile.
